speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape three of the interview with Mr. Hipsman, Hyme Hipsman, and I'm Amy Tar. Mr. Hipsman, you were-- you just told us that you were in several DP camps in Germany. What were they like?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were-- well, they weren't-- it was livable. It wasn't luxury. And it was livable. The houses wasn't built for luxury, you know, but nobody killed us. We survived there, you know? We had enough. Not too much, but enough food. You go out to make some-- some selling and buying things, and maybe earn some money. And we needed something, we went and bought ourselves. But it was very livable.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was in charge of them?

speaker: interviewee
text: In charge? The American, the American-- I don't know, or the Joint or the American government or whoever did it. I don't know. Somebody send in some food there.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: The Joint maybe, the HIAS or the American government, you know, The UNRRA? Could be the UNRRA. Yes, the UNRRA was in charge of that. I don't know but you-- you make it out, UNRRA. You-- I don't know. They used to call it UNRRA, you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: Right, the UN refugee organization. And you were telling us that you had a matchmaker set you up with your wife.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did you get married and when was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, what year were we getting married? In 1949? Yeah. And then in that camp where she stayed in [INAUDIBLE] got liquidated. And in that camp, [INAUDIBLE] where I stayed, got liquidated and both camps were sending into [INAUDIBLE]. So we made out of those two camps one.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we stayed together and in 1949, well, you-- we didn't stay together. I lived separate, you lived separate, of course. So since I got engaged to you already. So 1949, I think before the holidays, we got married. And-- and then a year after, my son Michael was born. And we went to Augsburg for legal immigration. And then we made out some papers and we-- we-- we went to America, you know? And the name of your wife?

speaker: interviewee
text: My wife, Ella.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was her maiden name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Maiden name, Citrin.

speaker: interviewer
text: How would you spell that?

speaker: interviewee
text: C-I-T-R-I-N.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, and so you came to the United States.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was-- that was after the winter. April? March?

speaker: interviewee
text: March 1950. March 1950, '51. And we came by plane to fly because where there's a child under certain amount, months old, they gave us a plane. We arrived to Kansas City. We came to Kansas City.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you leave from?

speaker: interviewee
text: We left from Munich, the airport in Munich. I don't know what the name of the airport. Munich.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you arrived in New York.

speaker: interviewee
text: We arrived in New York after two days of flight. We didn't flew with-- with the best plane, you know. With the-- what do you call it, what they send over.

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewee
text: How? Say it loud. Transport planes, you know. Transport planes. And we arrived to New York, New York, the airport. And from there, I guess somebody from the HIAS or from the Joint, they were taking us down and they gave us things and they put us up on a train and they send us away to Kansas City. Because my contact's a tailor. In Kansas City, there are a lot of tailor factories to work there.

speaker: interviewer
text: But when we arrived to Kansas City, I think somebody came and picked us up too, and they took us in right to a room-- rooms there. And everything was well-prepared and we were-- we were in America, you know, and good golden land, you know? So since then, I work and I made a nice family. And after a while, I left Kansas City. I came to Boston because my wife's parents came to Boston from-- from Europe, too, and she wanted to join them. So I didn't want to, but I still gave in, you know, and I decided to go to-- to Boston.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many years were you in Kansas City?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was-- it wasn't even a year. It was maybe half a year. One year? One year? Maybe a year.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you came to Boston.

speaker: interviewee
text: I came to Boston. And the beginning I struggled, you know, of course, you know? It was nothing brilliant, but later, since I went to work by myself it started out better, you know, and I'm happy.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you have a tailor shop.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I-- I have a tailor shop in Medford. I work by myself. I take in alterations and dry cleaning. And I'm there almost forty years, and I'm planning to retire soon, you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: And how many children do you have?

speaker: interviewee
text: I have three sons. The oldest is Michael Hipsman, and the middle one is Irwin Hipsman, and the youngest one is Folly Hipsman. And I have five-- five grandchildren. I have two grandsons from-- from Michael, my older son. I have two granddaughters from my middle son. And I have one granddaughter from the younger one, of Folly. I have five grandchildren. I'm happy. I live comfortable. I have my own house. I have a-- I have a car. My wife has a car. And it seems we are happy. The only thing is we're getting older, you know, and time is short.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Is there-- we're going to stop soon. I-- I was wondering if there's anything else that you didn't get a chance to say that you'd like to say about-- about your family and Poland, about the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: What else can I say? I think I told you everything there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell us why you agreed to do this interview?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because it's-- it's on my mind to speak it out. The only thing is I-- I-- went through too much that I should be forgotten that nobody will know about it. Because it's so much what I've been through. You just really hard to tell everybody, and they're not going to believe me that because it's an impossible thing.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's why I felt I'm very comfortable to give it out, you know? Let-- let somebody else know what was going on. That's my only idea. I-- I-- I don't hold back anything, and I don't tell any lie, and I tell all the truth how it happened, you know.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because I was hurt, because I was left for 15 years old without-- without a home, without parents, without brother and sister, and I was very hurt. That's why I decided that I have to give it, I have to tell the world about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you, Mr. Hipsman. Thank you.
